The **Bay of Plenty Region**[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-ReorgOrder2392-5) is a [local government region](/wiki/Regions_of_New_Zealand "Regions of New Zealand") in the [North Island](/wiki/North_Island "North Island") of New Zealand. Also called just the **Bay of Plenty** (**BOP**), it is situated around the [marine bight of that same name](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty "Bay of Plenty"). The bay was named by [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") after he noticed the abundant food supplies at several [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") villages there, in stark contrast to what he observed in [Poverty Bay](/wiki/Poverty_Bay "Poverty Bay").

The Bay of Plenty had an estimated resident population of 354,100 in June 2025,[[1]](#cite_note-NZ_population_data_2018-1) and is the fifth-most populous region in New Zealand.[[6]](#cite_note-Census_2023-6) It also has the third-highest regional population density in New Zealand, with only the 11th-largest land area. The major population centres are [Tauranga](/wiki/Tauranga "Tauranga"), [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua") and [Whakatāne](/wiki/Whakat%C4%81ne "Whakatāne"). The Bay of Plenty is one of the fastest growing regions in New Zealand: the regional population increased by 7.5% between 2001 and 2006,[[7]](#cite_note-Census2006-7) with significant growth in the coastal and western parts, and grew by 8.3% between 2018 and 2023. It has the second-largest [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") population in New Zealand, with over 30% of its population being of Māori descent, and the second-highest number of Māori speakers in the country. It also has the largest number of [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") of any region.[[8]](#cite_note-8) There are 35 local iwi, 260 [hapū](/wiki/Hap%C5%AB "Hapū") and 224 [marae](/wiki/Marae "Marae"), with the most significant iwi being [Te Arawa](/wiki/Te_Arawa "Te Arawa"), [Ngāi Tūhoe](/wiki/Ng%C4%81i_T%C5%ABhoe "Ngāi Tūhoe"), [Ngāti Awa](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Awa "Ngāti Awa") and [Te Whakatōhea](/wiki/Whakat%C5%8Dhea "Whakatōhea").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Significant horticultural, forestry and tourism industries are well established in the region. However, the Bay of Plenty is the third-most economically deprived region in New Zealand, with the eastern districts being among the least economically developed in the country.[[10]](#cite_note-RLTS-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bay_of_Plenty_Region&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Bay of Plenty region as a [local government area](/wiki/Regions_of_New_Zealand "Regions of New Zealand") was formed in the nationwide [1989 local government reforms](/wiki/1989_New_Zealand_local_government_reforms "1989 New Zealand local government reforms"). The new region incorporated the former counties of Tauranga, Rotorua, Whakātane and Ōpōtiki, and the towns of the same names.

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bay_of_Plenty_Region&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Governance")]

[Bay of Plenty Regional Council](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_Regional_Council "Bay of Plenty Regional Council") oversees regional land use, environmental management and civil defence.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The region wholly includes the [territorial authority](/wiki/Territorial_authorities_of_New_Zealand "Territorial authorities of New Zealand") areas of [Western Bay of Plenty District](/wiki/Western_Bay_of_Plenty_District "Western Bay of Plenty District"), [Tauranga City](/wiki/Tauranga "Tauranga"), [Whakatāne District](/wiki/Whakat%C4%81ne_District "Whakatāne District"), [Kawerau District](/wiki/Kawerau_District "Kawerau District"), and [Ōpōtiki District](/wiki/%C5%8Cp%C5%8Dtiki_District "Ōpōtiki District"), and parts of [Rotorua Lakes District](/wiki/Rotorua_Lakes_District "Rotorua Lakes District") and the town of [Rangitaiki](/wiki/Rangitaiki "Rangitaiki") in [Taupō District](/wiki/Taup%C5%8D_District "Taupō District").

Public health in New Zealand is broken into regions. The Bay of Plenty and Lakes [district health boards](/wiki/District_health_board "District health board") have public health provided by Toi Te Ora – Public Health.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/BayofPlentyA2002296.jpg/250px-BayofPlentyA2002296.jpg)](/wiki/File:BayofPlentyA2002296.jpg)

Satellite photo of the Bay of Plenty (top half of image)

The Bay of Plenty region covers 12,200 km2 (4,700 sq mi) of land and 9,500 km2 (3,700 sq mi) of coastal marine area.[[12]](#cite_note-EBOP-geo-12) It extends along the eastern coast of the North Island, from the base of the [Coromandel Peninsula](/wiki/Coromandel_Peninsula "Coromandel Peninsula") in the west to [Cape Runaway](/wiki/Cape_Runaway "Cape Runaway") in the east. The region extends 12 nautical miles from the mainland coastline, and also extends from the coastlines of several islands in the bay, notably [Mayor Island/Tuhua](/wiki/Mayor_Island/Tuhua "Mayor Island/Tuhua"), [Mōtītī Island](/wiki/M%C5%8Dt%C4%ABt%C4%AB_Island "Mōtītī Island"), [Whale Island](/wiki/Whale_Island,_New_Zealand "Whale Island, New Zealand") and the active volcano of [Whakaari/White Island](/wiki/Whakaari/White_Island "Whakaari/White Island"). It extends inland to the sparsely populated forest lands around Rotorua and [Murupara](/wiki/Murupara "Murupara").

The region has more than 200 square kilometres (77 sq mi) of lakes, known as the [Lakes of Rotorua](/wiki/Lakes_of_Rotorua "Lakes of Rotorua").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/WhakarewarewaGeyserFlat.jpg/250px-WhakarewarewaGeyserFlat.jpg)](/wiki/File:WhakarewarewaGeyserFlat.jpg)

Geothermal activity at [Whakarewarewa](/wiki/Whakarewarewa "Whakarewarewa")

Much of the central part of the region lies within the [Taupō Volcanic Zone](/wiki/Taup%C5%8D_Volcanic_Zone "Taupō Volcanic Zone"), which extends from the centre of the North Island northwards to Whakaari/White Island. Volcanic mountains and lakes, [geothermal](/wiki/Geothermal_activity "Geothermal activity") areas and geological fault lines all dot the landscape. The geothermal region around Rotorua is a major tourist site, while many hot springs in the region are used as swimming areas. The geothermal field near [Kawerau](/wiki/Kawerau "Kawerau") is the site of a [geothermal power plant](/wiki/Kawerau_Power_Station "Kawerau Power Station") that will reportedly meet up to one third of residential and industrial electricity demand in the Eastern Bay of Plenty.[[13]](#cite_note-MRP-13) Whakaari/White Island, the site of a former sulfur-mining operation, is an active volcanic island popular with tourists. The eruption of [Mount Tarawera](/wiki/Mount_Tarawera "Mount Tarawera") in 1886 and the [1987 Edgecumbe earthquake](/wiki/1987_Edgecumbe_earthquake "1987 Edgecumbe earthquake") were two disasters related to geological activity in the volcanic plateau.

Prominent volcanic cones in the region include [Mount Maunganui](/wiki/Mount_Maunganui "Mount Maunganui"), [Mount Tarawera](/wiki/Mount_Tarawera "Mount Tarawera") and [Mount Edgecumbe/Putauaki](/wiki/Mount_Edgecumbe/Putauaki "Mount Edgecumbe/Putauaki"). These features also have cultural significance to local Māori. The [Kaimai](/wiki/Kaimai_Ranges "Kaimai Ranges") and [Mamaku](/wiki/Mamaku_Ranges "Mamaku Ranges") mountain ranges lie at the western border of the region. Swamp land was formerly concentrated around a number of rivers, but much of this was dredged in the early part of the 20th century to increase land for settlement and other uses.[[14]](#cite_note-TeAra-14) Large native and foreign (planted) forest areas are found in the inland parts of the region. The [Kaingaroa Forest](/wiki/Kaingaroa_Forest "Kaingaroa Forest") is the world's largest planted forest, comprising [radiata pine](/wiki/Radiata_pine "Radiata pine") mainly used for timber.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bay_of_Plenty_Region&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

The Bay of Plenty region has warm, humid summers and mild winters. It is one of the warmest regions in New Zealand, particularly along the coastline, and most areas experience at least 2,200 hours of sunshine per annum. Average daily maximum temperatures range from 10 to 16 °C (50 to 61 °F) in winter and 22 to 26 °C (72 to 79 °F) in summer. Typical minima vary from 0 to 9 °C (32 to 48 °F) in winter and 11 to 17 °C (52 to 63 °F) during summer. Rainfall occurs more frequently in winter than in summer, but tropical storms in summer and autumn can produce heavy rain with high winds.[[15]](#cite_note-EBOP-climate-15) Central parts of the region can receive up to 2,000 millimetres (79 in) of rainfall annually, while the eastern and western areas can receive up to 4,000 millimetres (160 in).[[16]](#cite_note-TA-climate-16)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bay_of_Plenty_Region&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Bay of Plenty region covers 12,071.55 km2 (4,660.85 sq mi)[[17]](#cite_note-Area-17) and had an estimated population of 351,500 as of June 2025,[[1]](#cite_note-NZ_population_data_2018-1) with a population density of 29 people per km2.

Bay of Plenty region had a population of 334,140 in the [2023 New Zealand census](/wiki/2023_New_Zealand_census "2023 New Zealand census"), an increase of 25,641 people (8.3%) since the [2018 census](/wiki/2018_New_Zealand_census "2018 New Zealand census"), and an increase of 66,399 people (24.8%) since the [2013 census](/wiki/2013_New_Zealand_census "2013 New Zealand census"). There were 163,203 males, 170,004 females and 939 people of [other genders](/wiki/Non-binary_gender "Non-binary gender") in 120,057 dwellings.[[19]](#cite_note-19) 2.3% of people identified as [LGBTIQ+](/wiki/LGBTQ "LGBTQ"). The median age was 39.7 years (compared with 38.1 years nationally). There were 66,453 people (19.9%) aged under 15 years, 57,576 (17.2%) aged 15 to 29, 145,197 (43.5%) aged 30 to 64, and 64,911 (19.4%) aged 65 or older.[[6]](#cite_note-Census_2023-6)

People could identify as more than one ethnicity. The results were 71.9% [European](/wiki/European_New_Zealanders "European New Zealanders") ([Pākehā](/wiki/P%C4%81keh%C4%81 "Pākehā")); 30.6% [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people"); 4.3% [Pasifika](/wiki/Pasifika_New_Zealanders "Pasifika New Zealanders"); 8.8% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_New_Zealanders "Asian New Zealanders"); 1.2% Middle Eastern, Latin American and African New Zealanders (MELAA); and 2.1% other, which includes people giving their ethnicity as "New Zealander". English was spoken by 96.2%, Māori language by 9.0%, Samoan by 0.4% and other languages by 10.3%. No language could be spoken by 2.1% (e.g. too young to talk). [New Zealand Sign Language](/wiki/New_Zealand_Sign_Language "New Zealand Sign Language") was known by 0.4%. The percentage of people born overseas was 20.6, compared with 28.8% nationally.

Religious affiliations were 30.4% [Christian](/wiki/Christianity_in_New_Zealand "Christianity in New Zealand"), 1.3% [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism_in_New_Zealand "Hinduism in New Zealand"), 0.3% [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_New_Zealand "Islam in New Zealand"), 3.7% [Māori religious beliefs](/wiki/Religion_of_M%C4%81ori_people "Religion of Māori people"), 0.6% [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism_in_New_Zealand "Buddhism in New Zealand"), 0.4% [New Age](/wiki/New_Age "New Age"), 0.1% [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism_in_New_Zealand "Judaism in New Zealand"), and 2.7% other religions. People who answered that they had [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_New_Zealand "Irreligion in New Zealand") were 53.4%, and 7.4% of people did not answer the census question.

Of those at least 15 years old, 42,465 (15.9%) people had a bachelor's or higher degree, 145,284 (54.3%) had a post-high school certificate or diploma, and 66,879 (25.0%) people exclusively held high school qualifications. The median income was $39,000, compared with $41,500 nationally. 26,280 people (9.8%) earned over $100,000 compared to 12.1% nationally. The employment status of those at least 15 was that 129,156 (48.2%) people were employed full-time, 37,209 (13.9%) were part-time, and 9,216 (3.4%) were unemployed.[[6]](#cite_note-Census_2023-6)

The region has smaller populations of Pasifika and Asians than other regions.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/2023_NZ_Census_Population_Density_-_Bay_of_Plenty_Region.png/250px-2023_NZ_Census_Population_Density_-_Bay_of_Plenty_Region.png)](/wiki/File:2023_NZ_Census_Population_Density_-_Bay_of_Plenty_Region.png)

Population density map of the Bay of Plenty at the 2023 Census. Red = High, Green = Low.

The Bay of Plenty is the fifth-most populous region in New Zealand, accounting for 6.6% of the national population.[[1]](#cite_note-NZ_population_data_2018-1)

The coast is dotted with several sizeable settlements, the largest of which is the conurbation of the city of Tauranga and its neighbour [Mount Maunganui](/wiki/Mount_Maunganui "Mount Maunganui") in the west. The town of Whakatāne is located in the centre of the coast. Other towns of note include [Waihi Beach](/wiki/Waihi_Beach "Waihi Beach"), [Katikati](/wiki/Katikati "Katikati"), [Maketu](/wiki/Maketu "Maketu"), [Pukehina Beach](/wiki/Pukehina_Beach "Pukehina Beach") and [Ōpōtiki](/wiki/%C5%8Cp%C5%8Dtiki "Ōpōtiki").

Most of the population along the coast is concentrated in the western and central parts of the shore; the eastern part is sparsely populated hill country. The region has the third-highest regional population density in New Zealand, with only the 11th-largest land area. The major population centres are [Tauranga](/wiki/Tauranga "Tauranga"), [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua") and [Whakatāne](/wiki/Whakat%C4%81ne "Whakatāne").

Significant [horticultural](/wiki/Horticulture "Horticulture"), [forestry](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry") and [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism") industries are well established in the region. However, the Bay of Plenty is the third-most economically deprived region in New Zealand, with the eastern districts being among the least economically developed in the country.[[10]](#cite_note-RLTS-10)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bay_of_Plenty_Region&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Economy")]

The subnational gross domestic product (GDP) of Bay of Plenty was estimated at NZ$17.24 billion in the year to March 2019, 5.7% of New Zealand's national GDP. The subnational GDP per capita was estimated at $53,700 in the same period. In the year to March 2018, primary industries contributed $1.89 billion (11.6%) to the regional GDP, goods-producing industries contributed $3.24 billion (20.0%), service industries contributed $9.72 billion (59.8%), and taxes and duties contributed $1.39 billion (8.6%).[[22]](#cite_note-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Cruise_Ship_In_Port_Of_Tauranga.jpg/250px-Cruise_Ship_In_Port_Of_Tauranga.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cruise_Ship_In_Port_Of_Tauranga.jpg)

The [Port of Tauranga](/wiki/Port_of_Tauranga "Port of Tauranga") is the largest port in the country by cargo volume.

[Agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"), [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resource "Natural resource") and [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism") are the major industries. Most (96 per cent) of the region is defined as 'rural', with 22% of land usage representing farm land and 38% representing nature reserve land.[[10]](#cite_note-RLTS-10) The most common agricultural land uses are [horticulture](/wiki/Horticulture "Horticulture"), [dairy](/wiki/Dairy "Dairy"), [grazing](/wiki/Grazing "Grazing") and [sheep](/wiki/Sheep "Sheep") farming. The region has over 11,500 hectares of horticultural land, predominantly producing [kiwifruit](/wiki/Kiwifruit "Kiwifruit") and [avocadoes](/wiki/Avocado "Avocado").[[23]](#cite_note-23) The region also has an abundance of coastal, forestry and geothermal resources. Forestry emerged as a vital industry in the 1950s, with radiata pine being planted during the early 20th century. Forestry is commercially planted and managed, mostly using planted foreign tree species, and timber is sent to the [Port of Tauranga](/wiki/Port_of_Tauranga "Port of Tauranga") for export. Geothermal activity is a source of tourism, and geothermal energy is emerging as a major regional source of electricity. Tourism is the other notable industry, accounting for 15% of the region's GDP from March 2000 to 2004.[[24]](#cite_note-NZIER-24)

Overall economic growth in the Bay of Plenty averaged 2.1% between March 2000 and 2004, compared with the national rate of 3.5%, although per capita [real GDP](/wiki/Real_GDP "Real GDP") growth in the five years to March 2003 matched the national growth rate at an averaged 2.3%.[[24]](#cite_note-NZIER-24) In the 2013 Census, the median annual income was $26,200, below the national median of $28,500. Further, 39.3% of people aged 15 years or older earned an annual income of less than $20,000, compared with 38.2% of people nationally.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Unemployment was at 9.0% of people 15 years or older, compared with 7.1% nationally.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bay_of_Plenty_Region&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Tourism")]

The Bay of Plenty region is a popular holiday destination due to the warm and sunny summer climate and public beaches. The region received over 645,000 tourists in 2003, equivalent to one in three [visitors to New Zealand](/wiki/Tourism_in_New_Zealand "Tourism in New Zealand") coming to the region.[[24]](#cite_note-NZIER-24) Rotorua is a popular destination for international visitors, in particular the surrounding geothermal areas and Māori cultural centres. Tauranga is a popular domestic tourism destination, and also becoming popular internationally. [Whale watching](/wiki/Whale_watching "Whale watching") has become a popular attraction as the number of whales such as [blue whales](/wiki/Blue_whale "Blue whale") and [humpback whales](/wiki/Humpback_whale "Humpback whale") migrating into bay waters began to recover.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bay_of_Plenty_Region&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Transport")]

The Bay of Plenty region has 227 kilometres (141 mi) of rail network and 4,460 kilometres (2,770 mi) of roads.[[10]](#cite_note-RLTS-10) The main rail line is the [East Coast Main Trunk Railway](/wiki/East_Coast_Main_Trunk_Railway "East Coast Main Trunk Railway"), which extends from [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_New_Zealand "Hamilton, New Zealand") in the [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato "Waikato") region to Kawerau via Tauranga, with the Murupara Branch Railway extending the Kawerau terminus to Murupara, and the Mount Maunganui Branch connecting the Mount Maunganui terminus with the East Coast Main Trunk. The rail network is used exclusively for freight. The hub of regional economic activity is the Port of Tauranga, with well-established rail and road connections to other parts of the region. The three commercial airports are [Tauranga Airport](/wiki/Tauranga_Airport "Tauranga Airport"), [Rotorua Airport](/wiki/Rotorua_Airport "Rotorua Airport") and [Whakatāne Airport](/wiki/Whakat%C4%81ne_Airport "Whakatāne Airport").

Car travel remains the dominant form of transport in the region. In 2002, the number of vehicles owned in the region was 189,000, with an average of 1.51 vehicles per household.[[10]](#cite_note-RLTS-10) There are public transport bus services in Tauranga and Rotorua only. Significant growth in the Western Bay of Plenty District has seen increased strain on road infrastructure, particularly with increasing traffic congestion in Tauranga. A new highway network is being planned and constructed in Tauranga to join with its current network spanning on the western side of the city. The [NZ Transport Agency](/wiki/NZ_Transport_Agency "NZ Transport Agency"), in conjunction with Environment Bay of Plenty, Tauranga City and the Western Bay of Plenty District Councils, is planning to build an Eastern Motorway bypassing Te Puke, a Western Motorway bypassing [Ōmokoroa](/wiki/%C5%8Cmokoroa "Ōmokoroa") and a smaller Southern Motorway.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bay_of_Plenty_Region&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Sport")]

The Bay of Plenty is represented in several domestic sporting competitions. The [Bay of Plenty Rugby Union](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_Rugby_Union "Bay of Plenty Rugby Union") oversees the [Bay of Plenty Steamers](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_Steamers "Bay of Plenty Steamers"), who play in the [Mitre10 Cup](/wiki/Mitre_10_Cup "Mitre 10 Cup"). The Steamers are also a feeder club for the [Chiefs](/wiki/Chiefs_(rugby) "Chiefs (rugby)") who play in the [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby") competition. The [Waikato/Bay of Plenty Magic](/wiki/Waikato/Bay_of_Plenty_Magic "Waikato/Bay of Plenty Magic") compete in the [ANZ Championship](/wiki/ANZ_Championship "ANZ Championship") in [netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball"), having previously played in the [National Bank Cup](/wiki/National_Bank_Cup "National Bank Cup"). [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_cricket_team "Bay of Plenty cricket team") also makes up a part of the [Northern Districts](/wiki/Northern_Districts_Knights "Northern Districts Knights") cricket region and the Midlands hockey region.
